items.title: What do CDU and SPD executives say about the future of a potential government coalition? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Germany's voters have opted for a change of government and a shift to the right.  With results from Sunday's national election now official, the conservative Friedrich Merz is poised to become the next chancellor. Merz's centre-right CDU/CSU bloc secured the most votes by a clear margin, although smaller than he wanted at under 30%. The Conservatives are likely to form a coalition with the current Chancellor Olaf Scholz's Social Democrats, who came third. The far-right AFD took second place, but Merz refuses to work with them. 

Chapters
00:00 David McAllister, CDU party executive and head of the Foreign Affairs Committee of the European Parliament
05:30 Hans Brandt, DW Political Correspondent
09:30 Annika Klose, SPD, member of the German Parliament

items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The results of Germany‚Äôs election point to a two-party coalition. It would bring together the CDU, under whose leadership the current crisis began, and the SPD, under whose leadership things deteriorated. The billion-dollar-question: Can those two return the country‚Äôs economy to its old strength?

items.title: Is Friedrich Merz the man to fix Europe's broken backbone on the global stage? | To The Point
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Germany has decided. Olaf Scholz governing coalition crashed, the three parties ALL punished with historically low numbers. And the big winner is: 
Friedrich Merz and his center-right Christian Democrats. He must now forge a new coalition goverment and lead Europe's biggest economy out of its current crisis. He also needs to take the lead in Europe, countering an increasingly hostile White House. But the other big winner is the far-right AfD, nearly doubling its share of the vote to around 20 percent ‚Äď helped in no small measure by vocal support from Donald Trump and Elon Musk. But: Merz vowed he will under NO circumstance work with the far right. The question:  Friedrich Merz to the rescue ‚Äď is he the man to fix Europe's broken backbone on the global stage?

Kerstin M√ľnstermann, (Rheinische Post)
Gordon Repinski, (Executive Editor Germany for Politico)
Daniel Goffart, (Chief-Correspondent Europe for WirtschaftsWoche)

Chapters
00:00 Mood of change in Germany
03:00 Far-right AfD nearly doubled, pressure on new government
04:20 It was a mistake by Merz to vote together with AfD
05:20 Who is Friedrich Merz (video)
07:00 He is an experienced politician, but never held office
08:30 There is a gap between CDU and SPD; migration for example
10:00 How to organize the budget will be the big question 
11:00 Coalition talks will be difficult 
12:10 AfD‚Äôs  influence will be big, especially in eastern parts of Germany
13:30 AfD‚Äôs Alice Weidel: ‚ÄúStretching out our hand‚ÄĚ (Video)
14:05 There is a decent majority against AfD
15:00 If the next government is not successful, AfD will be on the rise 
16:00 Plans for boosting economy: cutting taxes and red tape 
17:20 The ‚Äúdebt brake‚ÄĚ will end; Merz is willing to invest 
21:05 Merz will be eye to eye with Trump
22:44 Merz election - not good news for Ukraine
23:28 Scholz was not a strong voice in the EU, Merz might be 
24:10 Merz has to take the German approach to the EU: just pay.
25:15 Merz is self confident 

#ToThePoint #FriedrichMerz #germanelections

items.title: Ukraine ceasefire would be 'a trap that will end in another conflict' Lithuania's FM | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Germany‚Äôs chancellor-in-waiting Friedrich Merz says achieving independence from the USA will be a priority, arguing Washington does not care about Europe‚Äôs fate. But asked about Merz‚Äô pledge, Lithuania‚Äôs foreign minister warned there is ‚Äúno substitute‚ÄĚ to a US defensive presence in Europe and said the EU should seek ‚Äúopportunities to strengthen bonds‚ÄĚ with the Trump administration.
‚ÄúEven if we start spending all of us 5% right now this year for our defense needs, we cannot build those capabilities even in five or ten years. We are dependent on transatlantic relations,‚ÄĚ Kńôstutis Budrys told DW in Brussels the day after Germany‚Äôs election.
‚ÄúIf NATO collapses, if all the security architecture collapses, everyone is in danger and everyone will face the existential threats,‚ÄĚ he said, adding that Germany would also be exposed.
Budrys reiterated a call for Germany to provide Ukraine with long-range weapons, something the outgoing administration in Berlin ruled out in a bid to avoid an escalation of the war. 
‚ÄúThat's what Ukraine needs, and not providing these tools for Ukraine, then we are lying to ourselves that we are supporting Ukraine until the victory,‚ÄĚ the minister said on the sidelines of EU talks on Monday.
Budrys was careful not to criticize the US for its attempt to broker a peace deal over Ukraine, but he issued a general warning about negotiating with Russia:
‚ÄúA ceasefire may look like [an] appealing way and then to have the quick result. But it's a trap that will end in another conflict, be it repeated aggression against Ukraine or be it another target in Europe,‚ÄĚ he said.
"We have to go in and talk and explain and make it right, because you cannot make the good decisions on the wrong assessments," he added. "We want Americans to be on the right side because I want to believe that they are trying to achieve what we are trying to achieve here: the just and lasting peace."


#Lithuania #NATO #Ukraine

items.title: What caused Germany's shift to the right, and what happens next? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Germans have voted for change with the country's conservatives claiming victory in Sunday's national election. With the votes now tallied, it has become clear that the only way to a majority for Friedrich Merz's Christian Democrats will be a coalition with the Social Democrats. The far-right AFD have come in second with a strong showing, but Merz refuses to work with them - as do all other German parties.

items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Germany's conservative bloc with Chancellor candidate Merz emerges as the strongest force from the early federal election. According to projections, the AfD comes in second place with high increases. The SPD plummets, but is ahead of the Greens. The Left can celebrate. The FDP and BSW are trembling.

items.title: Coalition talks to focus on German economy | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Breathing new life into the German economy will be a focal point of coalition talks, after voters said it was one of their top issues. High costs and growing uncertainty have wracked the country since the beginning of the war in Ukraine in 2022. Europe's largest economy has shrunk over the past two years, and job losses are mounting. Business groups are pushing for less bureaucracy and more competitive energy and labor costs. 

Chapters:
00:00 CDU/CSU win German election
02:41 Coalition talks to focus on German economy
03:15 Steven Beardsley, DW Reporter

items.title: Trump: Germans tired of 'no common sense agenda' | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>US President Donald Trump has celebrated the election result in a social media post, saying it was a great day for Germany and the US alike. Trump's confidant, tech billionaire Elon Musk, endorsed the far-right Alternative for Germany party ahead of the vote. Last month, Musk gave a virtual address at an AfD rally, saying quote 'Only the AfD can save Germany.' Friedrich Merz is critical of Musk's support for the AfD, saying it resembles election interference by Russia. 

Chapters:
00:00 Trump calls CDU victory 'a great day for Germany'
00:44 Benjamin Alvarez Gruber, DW Correspondent
